---
title: "DRoberts Case Study 1 DS6306"
author: "Diane Roberts"
date: "2026-02-21"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
  word_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

## Establish library calls
```{r library calls}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(tidyr)
library(knitr)
library(caret)
library(class)
library(e1071)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(magick)

```
## Ingest data for evaluation
```{r ingest data}

employee_data = read.csv("/Users/dianewroberts/Case Study 1/CaseStudy1-data.csv", sep = ",", header = TRUE)

head(employee_data)
str(employee_data)

names(employee_data)
```
## Data wrangling - Impute missing fields and ensure that data is categoriezed as needed
```{r data wrangling}

#Evaluate if there are blank or NA fields
employee_data %>%
  summarise(across(everything(),
                   ~sum(is.na(.) | . == "", na.rm = TRUE)))

#No blanks or NA fields located

employee_data_clean <- employee_data %>%
  mutate(Attrition_factor = as.factor(Attrition),
         BusinessTravel_factor = as.factor(BusinessTravel),
         Department_factor = as.factor(Department),
         Education_factor = as.factor(Education),
         EducationField_factor = as.factor(EducationField),
         EnvironmentSatisfaction_factor = as.factor(EnvironmentSatisfaction),
         Gender_factor = as.factor(Gender),
         JobInvolvement_factor = as.factor(JobInvolvement),
         JobLevel_factor = as.factor(JobLevel),
         JobSatisfaction_factor = as.factor(JobSatisfaction),
         MaritalStatus_factor = as.factor(MaritalStatus),
         OverTime_factor = as.factor(OverTime),
         PerformanceRating_factor = as.factor(PerformanceRating),
         RelationshipSatisfaction_factor = as.factor(RelationshipSatisfaction),
         StockOptionLevel_factor = as.factor(StockOptionLevel),
         WorkLifeBalance_factor = as.factor(WorkLifeBalance))

view(employee_data_clean)

# Create a dataset that only has employees who have left the company for visualization purposes

employee_attrition = employee_data_clean %>%
  filter(Attrition_factor == "Yes")

view(employee_attrition)

```
# Visualization of data

```{r data visualization, dev='png', fig.ext='png'}

## In 5 year increments determine the highest number of employees attriting by the total number of years working

ggplot(employee_attrition, aes(x = TotalWorkingYears)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)

# The histogram appears to indicate that employees are most likely to attrit at <12 years and there is a steep drop after that. This could be due to being more willing to move to another job earlier in career for money, title, etc and being less tied to benefits and company culture. The highest level of attrition appears to be at 4-7 years

# To view this smoothly I will create a density plot

ggplot(employee_attrition, aes(x = TotalWorkingYears)) +
  geom_density()

## Creating a dataset with those employees who have not attrited

#Original plot, worked to create an exec slide-friendly version
#employee_notattrition = employee_data_clean %>%
#  filter(Attrition_factor == "No")
#ggplot(employee_notattrition, aes(x = TotalWorkingYears)) +
#  geom_density()

cutoff <- 12

employee_notattrition <- employee_data_clean %>%
  filter(Attrition_factor == "No")

ggplot(employee_notattrition, aes(x = TotalWorkingYears)) +
  
  # Base density curve
  geom_density(fill = "gray85",
               color = "gray20",
               linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  # Highlight region under same density curve
  geom_density(
    aes(fill = after_stat(ifelse(x < cutoff, "highlight", "normal"))),
    alpha = 0.9,
    color = NA
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("highlight" = "darkorange",
                               "normal" = NA),
                    guide = "none") +
  
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff,
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 1) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Total Working Years Distribution",
    x = "Total Working Years",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black"),   # show numbers
    axis.ticks.y = element_line(color = "black")   # show ticks
  )

# Conduct a plot across all employees to determine age in years and highlight by color the difference with attrition versus non-attrition employees

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = Age, y = TotalWorkingYears)) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = Attrition_factor))

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = Age)) +
  geom_bar()

## In 5 year increments determine the highest number of employees attriting by the number of years working at DDSAnalytics

ggplot(employee_attrition, aes(x = YearsAtCompany)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)

## Now to evaluate the Attrition rate by Department

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Department, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Department",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Department")

plot_df <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Department_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(Department_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Department_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Department",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

# Do a comparison by Department for Business Travel and attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(BusinessTravel, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Business Travel",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Business Travel")

plot_df <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Department_factor, BusinessTravel_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(Department_factor, BusinessTravel_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = ifelse(prop > 0.05, percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1), "")
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df,
       aes(x = Department_factor,
           y = prop,
           fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 3.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ BusinessTravel_factor) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  theme_minimal()

# Now I would like to visualize the impact of travel on overall attrition; categorical x categorical

plot_df_3 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(BusinessTravel_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(BusinessTravel_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_3, aes(x = BusinessTravel_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Travel",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Next review Daily Salary Rate against Attrition
ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = DailyRate, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## Distance from home
ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = DistanceFromHome, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## Education

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Education, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Education",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Education")

plot_df_4 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Education_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(Education_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_4, aes(x = Education_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Education",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Education field

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(EducationField, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Education Field",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Education Field")

plot_df_5 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(EducationField_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(EducationField_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_5, aes(x = EducationField_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Education Field",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## EnvironmentSatisfaction

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(EnvironmentSatisfaction, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Environment Satisfaction",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Environment Satisfaction")

plot_df_6 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(EnvironmentSatisfaction_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(EnvironmentSatisfaction_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_6, aes(x = EnvironmentSatisfaction_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Environment Satisfaction",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

#Gender

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Gender, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Gender",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Gender")

plot_df_7 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(Gender_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(Gender_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_7, aes(x = Gender_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Gender",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Hourly Rate against Attrition
ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = HourlyRate, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## Job Involvement as related to Attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(JobInvolvement, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Job Involvement",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Job Involvement")

plot_df_8 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(JobInvolvement_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(JobInvolvement_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_8, aes(x = JobInvolvement_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Job Involvement",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Job Level related to Attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(JobLevel, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Job Level",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Job Level")

plot_df_9 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(JobLevel_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(JobLevel_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_9, aes(x = JobLevel_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Job Level",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Using Volume create a horizontal bar chart of employees who attrit

plot_left <- employee_data_clean %>%
  filter(Attrition_factor == "Yes") %>%
  count(JobLevel, name = "Left_Count") %>%
  mutate(
    JobLevel = factor(JobLevel, levels = 5:1)  # reverse order
  )

ggplot(plot_left, aes(x = JobLevel, y = Left_Count, fill = Left_Count)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Left_Count),
            hjust = -0.15,
            size = 6,
            fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#CFE8FF", high = "#0B3A66") +
  labs(
    title = "Attrition Volume by Job Level",
    subtitle = "Darker bars indicate higher attrition counts",
    x = "Job Level",
    y = "Employees Who Left"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  ) +
  expand_limits(y = max(plot_left$Left_Count) * 1.15)

## Job Satisfaction by Attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(JobSatisfaction, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Job Satisfaction",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Job Satisfaction")

plot_df_10 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(JobSatisfaction_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(JobSatisfaction_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_10, aes(x = JobSatisfaction_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Job Satisfaction",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Marital Status

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(MaritalStatus, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Marital Status",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Marital Status")

plot_df_11 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(MaritalStatus_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(MaritalStatus_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_11, aes(x = MaritalStatus_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Marital Status",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Over time work related to Attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(OverTime, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Over Time",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Over Time")

plot_df_12 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(OverTime_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(OverTime_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_12, aes(x = OverTime_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Over Time",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Percentage Increase related to Attrition

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = PercentSalaryHike, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## Performance Rating related to Attrition. This is an interesting self-reporting statistic as no employee in the dataset reported having a 1 or 2 rating.

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(PerformanceRating, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Performance Rating",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Performance Rating")

plot_df_13 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(PerformanceRating_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(PerformanceRating_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_13, aes(x = PerformanceRating_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Performance Rating",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Relationship Satisfaction related to Attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(RelationshipSatisfaction, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Relationship Satisfaction",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Relationship Satisfaction")

plot_df_14 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(RelationshipSatisfaction_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(RelationshipSatisfaction_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_14, aes(x = RelationshipSatisfaction_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Relationship Satisfaction",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Stock Option Level

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(StockOptionLevel, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Stock Option Level",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Stock Option Level")

plot_df_15 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(StockOptionLevel_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(StockOptionLevel_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_15, aes(x = StockOptionLevel_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Stock Option Level",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Training Times Last Year

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = TrainingTimesLastYear, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

##Work Life Balance related to Attrition

pivot_table <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(WorkLifeBalance, Attrition_factor) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Attrition_factor,
    values_from = n
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Total = No + Yes,
    Attrition_Rate = percent(Yes / Total, accuracy = 0.1)
  )

kable(pivot_table,
      col.names = c("Work Life Balance",
                    "Stayed (No)",
                    "Attrited (Yes)",
                    "Total Employees",
                    "Attrition Rate"),
      caption = "Attrition Rate by Work Life Balance")

plot_df_16 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  count(WorkLifeBalance_factor, Attrition_factor, name = "n") %>%
  group_by(WorkLifeBalance_factor) %>%
  mutate(
    prop  = n / sum(n),
    label = percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(plot_df_16, aes(x = WorkLifeBalance_factor, y = prop, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = label),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            color = "white",
            size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Attrition Rate by Work Life Balance",
       y = "Proportion",
       fill = "Attrition") +
  theme_minimal()

## Years in Current Role related to Attrition

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = YearsInCurrentRole, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## Years since last promotion

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = YearsSinceLastPromotion, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## Years with current manager

ggplot(employee_data_clean, aes(x = YearsWithCurrManager, fill = Attrition_factor)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Attrition_factor))

## These visualizations provide a path forward but do not deal with issues around the scale of the data and additional data wrangling that is needed to create models.

## Rank Variables by number of employees (volume) and probability in descending order

attrition_counts <- employee_data_clean %>%
  filter(Attrition == "Yes") %>%
  select(where(is.factor)) %>%   # only factor variables
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "Variable",
               values_to = "Value") %>%
  group_by(Variable, Value) %>%
  summarise(employees_lost = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(employees_lost))

# View the top attrition drivers
view(attrition_counts)

attrition_probability <- employee_data_clean %>%
  select(Attrition, where(is.factor)) %>%   # keep Attrition
  pivot_longer(cols = -Attrition,
               names_to = "Variable",
               values_to = "Value") %>%
  group_by(Variable, Value) %>%
  summarise(
    total_employees = n(),
    employees_lost = sum(Attrition == "Yes"),
    attrition_rate = employees_lost / total_employees,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(attrition_rate))

view(attrition_probability)

```
## Reviewing the impact of career stage on the top 3 attrition factors
```{r career stage}
attrition_profile_plot = employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    CareerStage = case_when(
      TotalWorkingYears <= 5 ~ "Early Career",
      TotalWorkingYears <= 10 ~ "Developing",
      TotalWorkingYears <= 20 ~ "Established",
      TRUE ~ "Senior"
    ),
    StockOptionGroup = ifelse(StockOptionLevel == 0, "No Stock Options", "Has Stock Options")
  ) %>%
  group_by(CareerStage, OverTime, StockOptionGroup) %>%
  summarise(
    AttritionRate = mean(Attrition_factor == "Yes"),
    Count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = CareerStage, y = AttritionRate, fill = OverTime)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~StockOptionGroup) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Attrition Risk by Employee Profile",
    subtitle = "Career Stage, Overtime, and Stock Incentives",
    x = "Career Stage",
    y = "Attrition Rate",
    fill = "Overtime"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

attrition_profile_plot

```
## Data Wrangling for k-NN model
```{r k-NN data prep}

## I kept the ordinal variables numeric and scaled because k-NN works off distance. If I dummy-encoded them, I’d increase the dimensionality and potentially weaken the calculations. This keeps the model simpler and more stable. And I should not scale based on the full data set but on each individual train and test data set to keep the information separate and not leak info.

## First dataset with full values to test the quality of the datasets
  kNN_employee_data <- employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    Attrition_factor = factor(Attrition_factor, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    OverTime_Yes = ifelse(OverTime == "Yes", 1, 0),
    MaritalStatus_Single = ifelse(MaritalStatus == "Single", 1, 0),
    Travel_Frequently = ifelse(BusinessTravel == "Travel_Frequently", 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Attrition_factor,
    Age,
    TotalWorkingYears,
    MonthlyIncome,
    StockOptionLevel,
    JobLevel,
    JobSatisfaction,
    JobInvolvement,
    WorkLifeBalance,
    OverTime_Yes,
    MaritalStatus_Single,
    Travel_Frequently)

names(kNN_employee_data)

 kNN_small_data <- employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    Attrition_factor = factor(Attrition_factor, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    OverTime_Yes = ifelse(OverTime == "Yes", 1, 0),
    MaritalStatus_Single = ifelse(MaritalStatus == "Single", 1, 0),
    Travel_Frequently = ifelse(BusinessTravel == "Travel_Frequently", 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(
  Attrition_factor,
  StockOptionLevel,
  JobLevel,
  OverTime_Yes,
  MonthlyIncome,
  Age
)
 
 view(kNN_small_data)

## Scale data
kNN_data <- kNN_employee_data %>%
  mutate(across(-Attrition_factor, scale))

kNN_data2 <- kNN_small_data %>%
  mutate(across(-Attrition_factor,scale))

## Create test and train datasets 
set.seed(123)
trainIndices <- createDataPartition(
  employee_data_clean$Attrition_factor,
  p = 0.70,
  list = FALSE
)

trainData_knn <- kNN_data[trainIndices, ]
testData_knn  <- kNN_data[-trainIndices, ]

head(trainData_knn)
head(testData_knn)

names(trainData_knn)

trainData_knn2 <- kNN_data2[trainIndices, ]
testData_knn2  <- kNN_data2[-trainIndices, ]

head(trainData_knn2)
head(testData_knn2)

names(trainData_knn2)


knn_k5 <- train(
  Attrition_factor ~ .,
  data = trainData_knn,
  method = "knn",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 5)
)

knn_k5

knn_k5b <- train(
  Attrition_factor ~ .,
  data = trainData_knn2,
  method = "knn",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 5)
)

knn_k5b

pred_class <- predict(knn_k5, newdata = testData_knn)
confusionMatrix(pred_class, testData_knn$Attrition_factor, positive = "Yes")

pred_class <- predict(knn_k5b, newdata = testData_knn2)
confusionMatrix(pred_class, testData_knn2$Attrition_factor, positive = "Yes")

knn_tuned <- train(
  Attrition_factor ~ .,
  data = trainData_knn2,
  method = "knn",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 1:30)
)

knn_tuned$bestTune
plot(knn_tuned)


pred_class <- predict(knn_tuned, newdata = testData_knn2)
confusionMatrix(pred_class, testData_knn2$Attrition_factor, positive = "Yes")


pred_prob <- predict(knn_tuned, newdata = testData_knn2, type = "prob")[, "Yes"]
thresholds <- seq(0.05, 0.60, by = 0.01)

# Based on the initial results of the full and smaller knn datasets, I have chosen to use the smaller one to reduce noise.

knn_results <- data.frame(threshold = thresholds, Sensitivity = NA, Specificity = NA, Accuracy = NA, TP = NA, FP = NA, TN = NA, FN = NA)

for (i in seq_along(thresholds)) {
  t <- thresholds[i]
  preds <- factor(ifelse(pred_prob >= t, "Yes", "No"), levels = c("No","Yes"))
  cm <- confusionMatrix(preds, testData_knn2$Attrition_factor, positive = "Yes")
  tab <- cm$table
  knn_results$Sensitivity[i] <- unname(cm$byClass["Sensitivity"])
  knn_results$Specificity[i] <- unname(cm$byClass["Specificity"])
  knn_results$Accuracy[i]    <- unname(cm$overall["Accuracy"])
  knn_results$TN[i] <- tab["No","No"];  knn_results$TP[i] <- tab["Yes","Yes"]
  knn_results$FP[i] <- tab["Yes","No"]; knn_results$FN[i] <- tab["No","Yes"]
}

knn_results

ggplot(knn_results, aes(x = threshold)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Sensitivity, color = "Sensitivity"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Specificity, color = "Specificity"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.60, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Sensitivity & Specificity by Threshold",
       x = "Threshold",
       y = "Metric Value",
       color = "Metric") +
  theme_minimal()

# This line of code calculates Balanced Accuracy
knn_results$BalancedAccuracy <- 
  (knn_results$Sensitivity + knn_results$Specificity) / 2

knn_results[which.max(knn_results$BalancedAccuracy), ]


knn_k23 <- train(
  Attrition_factor ~ .,
  data = trainData_knn2,
  method = "knn",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 23)
)

prob_yes_knn <- predict(knn_k23,
                        newdata = testData_knn2,
                        type = "prob")[, "Yes"]

threshold <- 0.09
# Used the threshold found by Balanced Accuracy

pred_knn_adj <- factor(ifelse(prob_yes_knn >= threshold, "Yes", "No"),
                       levels = c("No", "Yes"))

kNNcm <- confusionMatrix(pred_knn_adj,
                testData_knn2$Attrition_factor,
                positive = "Yes")

print("kNN Confusion Matrix")
kNNcm

```
## Now work on the Naive Bayes model for comparison
```{r nb data and model}

#Data wrangling - while not removing as many variables due to noise, I am removing variables that are constant and don't appear to add value. e.g, Over18 is always Yes. I also considered removing PerformanceRating because it is only ever 3 or 4 but decided against that.

employee_nb <- employee_data %>%
  select(
    Attrition,
    OverTime,
    JobLevel,
    JobInvolvement,
    MaritalStatus,
    TotalWorkingYears,
    WorkLifeBalance,
    MonthlyIncome,
    Age,
    BusinessTravel
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Attrition = factor(Attrition, levels = c("No","Yes")),
    OverTime = factor(OverTime),
    JobLevel = factor(JobLevel),        
    JobInvolvement = factor(JobInvolvement),
    MaritalStatus = factor(MaritalStatus),
    WorkLifeBalance = factor(WorkLifeBalance),
    BusinessTravel = factor(BusinessTravel)
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

trainData_nb <- employee_nb[trainIndices, ]
testData_nb  <- employee_nb[-trainIndices, ]

nb_model <- naiveBayes(Attrition ~ ., data = trainData_nb, laplace = 1)

prob_yes <- predict(nb_model,
                    newdata = testData_nb,
                    type = "raw")[, "Yes"]

threshold <- 0.11
pred_nb_adj <- factor(ifelse(prob_yes >= threshold, "Yes", "No"),
                   levels = c("No","Yes"))

nbcm <- confusionMatrix(pred_nb_adj,
                testData_nb$Attrition,
                positive = "Yes")
print("Naive Bayes Confusion Matrix:")
nbcm
## kNN and Naive Bayes are very close
```
## Calculate the cost for each model
```{r cost calculations}

retention_Incentive = 200.00

kNN_retention_Cost = retention_Incentive * (kNNcm$table[2,2] + kNNcm$table[2,1])
nb_retention_Cost = retention_Incentive * (nbcm$table[2,2] + nbcm$table[2,1])
print("kNN Retention Cost:")
kNN_retention_Cost
print("Naive Bayes Retention Cost:")
nb_retention_Cost

cost_results <- data.frame(
  Model = c("kNN", "Naive Bayes"),
  TotalCost = c(kNN_retention_Cost, nb_retention_Cost)
)

ggplot(cost_results, aes(x = Model, y = TotalCost, fill = Model)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(TotalCost, 0)),
            vjust = -0.5,
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("kNN" = "blue",
                               "Naive Bayes" = "red")) +
  labs(title = "Total Attrition Cost by Model",
       x = "Model",
       y = "Total Estimated Cost") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

## This makes it appear that the kNN model is best. However, we haven't evaluated total savings yet.

```
## Calculate total savings
## A key component in the savings model is that it says that the cost to save an employee can be between 50-400% of their MonthlyIncome annualized. 
# Replacement cost assumptions were modeled between 0.5× and 4× annual salary, consistent with research from SHRM (2017) and Boushey & Glynn (2012), which estimate turnover costs ranging from 50% to over 200% of annual compensation depending on role complexity. 
# Boushey, H., & Glynn, S. J. (2012). There are significant business costs to replacing employees. Center for American Progress.
# Society for Human Resource Management. (2017). Human Capital Benchmarking Report.I will calculate at .5x, 1x, 2x and 4x

```{r calculate total savings}

trainData <- kNN_data2[trainIndices, ]
testData  <- kNN_data2[-trainIndices, ]

testData$AnnualIncome <- testData$MonthlyIncome * 12

calculate_savings <- function(predictions, actuals, income, multiplier) {
  
  TP_rows <- which(predictions == "Yes" & actuals == "Yes")
  FN_rows <- which(predictions == "No"  & actuals == "Yes")
  
  TP_income <- sum(income[TP_rows])
  FN_income <- sum(income[FN_rows])
  
  retention_cost <- sum(predictions == "Yes") * 200
  
  money_saved <- TP_income * multiplier
  replacement_cost <- FN_income * multiplier
  
  net_savings <- money_saved - retention_cost - replacement_cost
  
  return(net_savings)
}

multipliers <- c(0.5, 1, 2, 4)

knn_results <- sapply(multipliers, function(m) {
  calculate_savings(
    pred_knn_adj,
    testData$Attrition_factor,
    testData$AnnualIncome,
    m
  )
})

nb_results <- sapply(multipliers, function(m) {
  calculate_savings(
    pred_nb_adj,
    testData$Attrition,
    testData$MonthlyIncome * 12,
    m
  )
})

financial_results <- data.frame(
  Multiplier = multipliers,
  kNN_NetSavings = knn_results,
  NB_NetSavings = nb_results
)

financial_long <- financial_results %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(kNN_NetSavings, NB_NetSavings),
    names_to = "Model",
    values_to = "NetSavings"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    NetLoss = -NetSavings,
    Model = recode(Model,
                   kNN_NetSavings = "k-NN",
                   NB_NetSavings  = "Naive Bayes")
  )

ggplot(financial_long,
       aes(x = Multiplier,
           y = NetLoss,
           color = Model,
           group = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 2) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = multipliers,
    labels = paste0(multipliers, "x")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Naive Bayes" = "#1F4E79",  # Blue
      "k-NN"        = "#C00000"   # Red
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Net Financial Impact by Model",
    x = "Replacement Cost Multiplier (0.5x to 4.0x)",
    y = "Net Loss ($)"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "top"
  )

## Across all replacement cost scenarios, from 0.5× to 4× annual salary, the Naive Bayes model consistently results in lower net financial loss than k-NN. As the assumed cost of replacing an employee increases, the total loss grows significantly, reinforcing that missed attrition cases (false negatives) are the primary financial driver. In higher-cost scenarios, the economic impact of failing to identify employees who leave outweighs the cost of retention interventions, making model sensitivity increasingly critical to financial performance.

## Now pair this data with the career stage information found earlier to get a view of the career stage, multipliers and replacement costs. Note that this is no longer the median salary but an attempt to look at various salary levels categorized by Job Level to see the impact.

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)

employee_data_career <- employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    CareerStage = case_when(
      JobLevel == 1 ~ "Entry",
      JobLevel == 2 ~ "Developing",
      JobLevel == 3 ~ "Mid-Senior",
      JobLevel >= 4 ~ "Leadership"
    )
  )

salary_stage <- employee_data_career %>%
  group_by(CareerStage) %>%
  summarise(
    MedianSalary = median(MonthlyIncome, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

multipliers <- tibble(
  Multiplier = c(0.5, 1, 2, 4)
)

cost_table <- crossing(salary_stage, multipliers) %>%
  mutate(
    AttritionCost = MedianSalary * 12 * Multiplier,
    CareerStage = factor(CareerStage,
                         levels = c("Entry","Developing","Mid-Senior","Leadership")),
    Multiplier = factor(Multiplier,
                        levels = c(0.5,1,2,4),
                        labels = c("0.5x","1x","2x","4x"))
  )

# Create label data for only the lowest and highest scenarios
label_data <- cost_table %>%
  filter(Multiplier %in% c("0.5x","4x"))

ggplot(cost_table, aes(x = CareerStage, y = AttritionCost, fill = Multiplier)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  geom_text(
  data = label_data,
  aes(label = label_dollar(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K", accuracy = 1)(AttritionCost)),
  position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
  angle = 90,
  vjust = -0.3,
  hjust = 0.5,
  size = 3.5,
  fontface = "bold"
) +
  scale_y_continuous(
  labels = dollar_format(),
  expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.18))
) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Produce an executive level summary
```{r executive summary table}

exec_table <- employee_data %>%
  count(Attrition) %>%
  mutate(
    Status = ifelse(Attrition == "No",
                    "Retained (Stayed)",
                    "Attrition (Lost)"),
    Percentage = percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  select(Status, Count = n, Percentage) %>%
  bind_rows(
    data.frame(
      Status = "Total Employees",
      Count = sum(.$Count),
      Percentage = "100%"
    )
  )


exec_table %>%
  kable(
    col.names = c("Status", "Count", "Percentage"),
    align = c("l", "c", "c")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    position = "left",
    font_size = 16
  ) %>%
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%      # Retained row bold
  row_spec(2,
           bold = TRUE,
           background = "#FDECEC",
           color = "#B22222") %>%   # Attrition row highlight
  row_spec(3, bold = TRUE) %>%      # Total row bold
  column_spec(2, bold = TRUE) %>%   # Bold Count column
  column_spec(3, bold = TRUE)

# Create a circular chart

attr_rate <- employee_data %>%
  summarise(rate = mean(Attrition == "Yes")) %>%
  pull(rate)

# Order matters: Stayed first, Attrited second
donut_df <- data.frame(
  category = c("Stayed", "Attrited"),
  value = c(1 - attr_rate, attr_rate)
)

ggplot(donut_df, aes(x = 2, y = value, fill = category)) +
  geom_col(color = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  # Rotate so orange lands bottom-left
  coord_polar(theta = "y", start = pi * 0.35) +
  
  xlim(0.5, 2.5) +  # controls hole size
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Stayed" = "#0B3A66",     # navy
    "Attrited" = "#D97706"    # dark orange
  )) +
  
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0,
           label = percent(attr_rate, accuracy = 0.1),
           size = 18, fontface = "bold") +
  
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 0,
           label = "\nAttrition Rate",
           size = 6)

```
## Charts for presentation Purposes
```{r presentation charts}

## Create a data frame with the top 3 attrition risks: Stock Options, OverTime and Job Level


# ---- 1) Build the 5 segments (adjust thresholds if your EDA used different cutoffs) ----
risk_df <- bind_rows(

  # 1) Zero Stock Options
  employee_data_clean %>%
    filter(StockOptionLevel == 0) %>%
    summarise(RiskFactor = "Zero Stock Options",
              Left = sum(Attrition_factor == "Yes")),

  # 2) Job Level 1 only (Entry Level)
  employee_data_clean %>%
    filter(JobLevel == 1) %>%
    summarise(RiskFactor = "Job Level 1\n(Entry Level)",
              Left = sum(Attrition_factor == "Yes")),

  # 3) Mandatory OverTime
  employee_data_clean %>%
    filter(OverTime == "Yes") %>%
    summarise(RiskFactor = "Mandatory OverTime",
              Left = sum(Attrition_factor == "Yes")),

  # 4) Low Environment Satisfaction (commonly 1 = low)
  employee_data_clean %>%
    filter(EnvironmentSatisfaction == 1) %>%
    summarise(RiskFactor = "Low Environment\nSatisfaction",
              Left = sum(Attrition_factor == "Yes")),

  # 5) Frequent Travel
  employee_data_clean %>%
    filter(BusinessTravel == "Travel_Frequently") %>%
    summarise(RiskFactor = "Frequent Travel",
              Left = sum(Attrition_factor == "Yes"))

) %>%
  mutate(
    # Top 3 = red; bottom 2 = gray (like the slide)
    Tier = ifelse(RiskFactor %in% c("Zero Stock Options",
                                   "Job Level 1\n(Entry Level)",
                                   "Mandatory OverTime"),
                  "Top", "Lower"),
    BarColor = ifelse(Tier == "Top", "#C81E1E", "#5A5A5A"),
    Label = ifelse(Tier == "Top",
                   paste0(Left, " Employees Lost"),
                   "Lower Volume")
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Left)) %>%
  mutate(RiskFactor = factor(RiskFactor, levels = rev(RiskFactor)))

# ---- 2) Plot (slide-style) ----
p <- ggplot(risk_df, aes(x = Left, y = RiskFactor)) +

  geom_col(fill = risk_df$BarColor, width = 0.65) +

  # Label inside top bars (white), "Lower Volume" outside gray bars (black)
  geom_text(aes(label = Label),
            color = ifelse(risk_df$Tier == "Top", "white", "black"),
            fontface = "bold",
            size = 5,
            hjust = ifelse(risk_df$Tier == "Top", 1, 0),
            nudge_x = ifelse(risk_df$Tier == "Top", -2, 2)) +

  labs(
    title = "Top Attrition Risk Segments",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  ) +

  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 34, color = "black"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 18, color = "black", face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#E8F0F4", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#E8F0F4", color = NA),
    plot.margin = margin(20, 40, 20, 40)
  ) +

  # Give extra room for "Employees Lost" text to the right
  expand_limits(x = max(risk_df$Left) * 1.25)

p
```
## Show the difference between cost and true cost

```{r cost vs true cost}

# 1) Build annual salary + scenario costs
cost_df <- employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(AnnualSalary = MonthlyIncome * 12) %>%
  crossing(multiplier = c(0.5, 1, 2, 4)) %>%
  mutate(ReplacementCost = AnnualSalary * multiplier,
         IncentiveCost = 200)

# 2) Summarize for boxplot look (distribution)
ggplot(cost_df, aes(x = factor(multiplier), y = ReplacementCost)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 200, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Replacement Cost vs. $200 Retention Investment",
    subtitle = "Replacement cost shown across employee salaries at 0.5×–4× annual salary",
    x = "Replacement Cost Multiplier (× Annual Salary)",
    y = "Estimated Replacement Cost"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16)

median_salary <- employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(AnnualSalary = MonthlyIncome * 12) %>%
  summarise(med_salary = median(AnnualSalary, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(med_salary)

median_table <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("0.5× Salary", "1× Salary", "2× Salary", "4× Salary"),
  `Replacement Cost` = dollar(median_salary * c(0.5, 1, 2, 4))
)

median_table %>%
  kable(
    align = "lc",
    caption = "Estimated Replacement Cost (Median Salary)"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    full_width = FALSE,
    font_size = 20,
    position = "center"
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#FDECEC")
```
## Table ready confusion matrix
```{r confusion matrix table}

cm_scorecard <- function(cm_obj, model_name = "Model") {
  tab <- cm_obj$table
  TP <- tab["Yes","Yes"]
  TN <- tab["No","No"]
  FP <- tab["Yes","No"]
  FN <- tab["No","Yes"]

  tibble(
    Model = model_name,
    TP = TP, FP = FP, TN = TN, FN = FN,
    Accuracy = percent(unname(cm_obj$overall["Accuracy"]), 0.1),
    Sensitivity = percent(unname(cm_obj$byClass["Sensitivity"]), 0.1),
    Specificity = percent(unname(cm_obj$byClass["Specificity"]), 0.1),
    Precision = percent(unname(cm_obj$byClass["Pos Pred Value"]), 0.1)
  )
}

score_tbl <- bind_rows(
  cm_scorecard(kNNcm, "k-NN (k=23, t=0.09)"),
  cm_scorecard(nbcm,  "Naive Bayes (t=0.11)")
)

kable(score_tbl, caption = "Model Confusion Matrix Scorecard") %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 18) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)

```

## Competition output for new csv file
```{r competition code}
comp_data = read.csv("/Users/dianewroberts/Case Study 1/CaseStudy1CompSet No Attrition.csv", sep = ",", header = TRUE)

head(comp_data)
str(comp_data)

names(comp_data)

# Retrain the knn model

kNN_small_data2 <- employee_data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    Attrition_factor = factor(Attrition_factor, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    OverTime_Yes = ifelse(OverTime == "Yes", 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Attrition_factor,
    StockOptionLevel,
    JobLevel,
    OverTime_Yes,
    MonthlyIncome,
    Age
  )

set.seed(123)
trainIndices <- createDataPartition(
  kNN_small_data2$Attrition_factor,
  p = 0.70,
  list = FALSE
)

trainData_knn3 <- kNN_small_data2[trainIndices, ]
testData_knn3  <- kNN_small_data2[-trainIndices, ]

knn_k23b <- train(
  Attrition_factor ~ .,
  data = trainData_knn3,
  method = "knn",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 23)
)

library(dplyr)

comp_data_clean <- comp_data %>%
  mutate(
    OverTime_Yes = ifelse(OverTime == "Yes", 1, 0),
    MaritalStatus_Single = ifelse(MaritalStatus == "Single", 1, 0),
    Travel_Frequently = ifelse(BusinessTravel == "Travel_Frequently", 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(
    ID, # Keep ID for the final output order
    TotalWorkingYears,
    MonthlyIncome,
    Age,
    StockOptionLevel,
    JobSatisfaction,
    OverTime_Yes,
    MaritalStatus_Single,
    Travel_Frequently,
    JobInvolvement,
    WorkLifeBalance,
    JobLevel
  )

comp_data_clean_scaled <- comp_data_clean %>%
  mutate(across(-ID, scale))

prob_yes_comp <- predict(knn_k23b, newdata = comp_data_clean, type = "prob")[, "Yes"]
comp_predictions <- ifelse(prob_yes_comp >= 0.09, "Yes", "No")

submission_df <- data.frame(
  ID = comp_data_clean$ID,
  Attrition = comp_predictions
)

submission_df <- submission_df[order(submission_df$ID), ]

write.csv(submission_df, "/Users/dianewroberts/Case Study 1/Case1PredictionsRoberts Attrition.csv", row.names = FALSE)

```
